skill.name: azure-enterprise-infra-planner
skill.description: Plan enterprise Azure infrastructure from workload descriptions, including networking, identity, security, compliance, and multi-resource topologies. The skill guides research, plan creation, Bicep or Terraform generation, validation, and deployment gates.
skill.summary: Plan Azure enterprise infrastructure and generate deployment-ready Bicep or Terraform guidance.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer over-counted Markdown backticks and Azure documentation links as executable Ruby, weak crypto, and network callbacks. However, SKILL.md contains an explicit authority and supersession block that attempts to override other guidance, and the skill directs agents to run Azure deployment commands after approval gates. This should not be published until the prompt-injection language is removed and deployment behavior is clearly sandboxed.

critical_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
critical_findings.description: SKILL.md claims authoritative mandatory compliance and says its guidance supersedes all other sources, including model training. This is an explicit attempt to elevate skill content above the host system and marketplace review instructions.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious language directly matches prompt-injection indicators: authoritative wording, mandatory compliance, and superseding other instructions. It appears in the top-level skill instructions that an assistant would load before acting.

high_findings.title: AI-Directed Azure Deployment Commands
high_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to execute Azure CLI and Terraform deployment commands after approval gates. These commands are legitimate for the skill purpose, but they can create or modify cloud resources when credentials are available.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deployment commands are explicit and semantically tied to the workflow. The files include approval gates, so this is high operational risk rather than confirmed malicious execution.

medium_findings.title: Mandatory External Tool and Documentation Access
medium_findings.description: The workflow requires Azure MCP calls, Microsoft documentation fetches, and sub-agent summarization. This network access is expected for current Azure guidance, but it increases data exposure and supply-chain risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is documented and appears legitimate for Azure planning. It still requires review because user architecture details may be sent to external tools or fetched sources.

medium_findings.title: Project Hidden Directory State Files
medium_findings.description: The skill writes and later updates a hidden project path for the infrastructure plan. The static Azure-credentials finding is partly false positive because the path is project-local, but hidden state files can contain sensitive architecture details.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct evidence of hidden project-directory use, but no evidence found that the skill reads the Azure CLI credential directory. The concern is data handling, not credential theft.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are Markdown inline code, tables, or fenced examples. They are not Ruby backtick execution in source code, although some examples document real CLI commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown documentation and code fences, not Ruby files or executable scripts. The classification is false positive, but the documented commands are separately assessed.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs and IP Ranges Are Reference Data
low_findings.description: The network detections are Microsoft Learn documentation URLs and Azure networking constraints such as default routes. No evidence found of callbacks, telemetry, or exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to Azure documentation, and the IP-like values describe routing rules. They are static reference content, not network code.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Pattern Matches Are Azure SKU Text
low_findings.description: Weak-crypto findings appear to be triggered by Azure SKU, route, or descriptive text in Markdown tables. No evidence found of implemented cryptographic algorithms or secret handling code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are architecture constraints and SKU descriptions, not cryptographic implementation. This supports dismissing those static matches as false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Instruction Hierarchy Override Language
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses authority language that can cause an assistant to prioritize the skill over system, developer, or marketplace-review instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly says the skill is canonical and supersedes all other sources. That is a direct instruction hierarchy risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud Resource Mutation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The deployment guide includes az deployment and terraform apply commands that can mutate Azure resources if executed with an authenticated account.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are shown as deployment steps, not passive references. Approval checks reduce but do not remove the risk.

content.value_statement: Enterprise Azure planning requires current service guidance, clear constraints, and careful deployment gates. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce infrastructure plans, Bicep, Terraform, and validation steps from workload requirements.
actual_capabilities: Plans Azure landing zones, hub-spoke networks, and multi-region topologies from workload descriptions.
Guides Well-Architected Framework research using Azure MCP tools and Microsoft Learn references.
Creates an infrastructure plan file with resources, dependencies, tradeoffs, and references.
Generates Bicep or Terraform file layouts after plan approval.
Checks resource pairing constraints for networking, compute, data, messaging, monitoring, and security services.
Provides validation and deployment steps for Azure CLI and Terraform workflows.
limitations: The reference catalog can lag behind current Azure service changes.
Deployment steps require Azure credentials, subscription access, and explicit user approval.
Generated plans still need human review for compliance, cost, and production readiness.
The skill contains prompt-injection style authority language and should be remediated before publication.

use_cases.title: Plan a landing zone
use_cases.description: Create a structured Azure landing zone plan with networking, identity, monitoring, security, and deployment tradeoffs.

use_cases.title: Prepare infrastructure as code
use_cases.description: Turn an approved infrastructure plan into Bicep or Terraform files with validation steps and clear file placement.

use_cases.title: Review service pairings
use_cases.description: Check Azure resource combinations for subnet, SKU, identity, private endpoint, and monitoring constraints before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a basic Azure plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new workload
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan Azure infrastructure for a small production web app with managed identity, private data access, monitoring, and cost-aware defaults. Stop before generating IaC.

prompt_templates.title: Design a hub-spoke network
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a hub-spoke Azure network for three application teams. Include firewall, private endpoints, DNS, route tables, and approval gates before deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Terraform from an approved plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the approved infrastructure plan to generate Terraform file structure and validation steps. Do not run apply until I explicitly confirm deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a multi-region platform
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Azure platform plan for multi-region reliability, least privilege, private networking, observability, and Well-Architected Framework gaps.

output_examples.input: Plan infrastructure for a regulated analytics platform on Azure.
output: A resource plan grouped by networking, identity, storage, analytics, monitoring, and security.
A list of dependencies, tradeoffs, and approval gates before IaC generation.
Recommended validation checks for naming, regions, SKUs, and private access.

output_examples.input: Generate deployment guidance for an approved hub-spoke design.
output: A Bicep or Terraform file layout with module responsibilities.
A validation sequence for syntax and deployment preview.
A reminder to require explicit confirmation before resource changes.

output_examples.input: Check whether these Azure resources can be paired safely.
output: A constraint review covering subnet rules, SKU compatibility, identity, monitoring, and private endpoint dependencies.
A short list of blockers and design changes to resolve them.

best_practices: Require explicit user approval before generating IaC or running deployment commands.
Verify Azure service limits, regions, SKUs, and naming rules against current Microsoft documentation.
Store secrets in managed services and keep generated plans free of real credentials.
anti_patterns: Do not let skill instructions override system, developer, or organizational security policies.
Do not run Terraform apply or Azure deployment commands from a draft plan.
Do not treat generated architecture as production-approved without human review.

faq.question: What does this skill produce?
faq.answer: It produces Azure infrastructure plans, resource dependency guidance, validation steps, and Bicep or Terraform structure.

faq.question: Can it deploy resources?
faq.answer: It includes deployment instructions, but deployment should require explicit approval and authenticated Azure access.

faq.question: Does it use Microsoft documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It directs the assistant to use Azure MCP tools and Microsoft Learn references for current guidance.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish as reviewed?
faq.answer: No. The audit found prompt-injection style authority language that should be removed before publication.

faq.question: Does it contain real credentials?
faq.answer: No evidence found of real credentials in the reviewed files. Some examples include placeholders for sensitive values.

faq.question: Who should use it?
faq.answer: Cloud architects, platform engineers, and DevOps reviewers who design or validate Azure infrastructure.
